title: Medical Equipment / Mobility Retail (101 Mobility)
verdict: Franchise Conditional — 101 Mobility is one of the few home-modification franchises with validated unit economics and SBA-friendly financing.
verdict_detail: The aging-in-place thesis is structural (10,000 Americans turn 65 every day). Mobility equipment — stair lifts, ramps, patient lifts, auto lifts — is a needs-based, often insurance-reimbursed purchase with high referral dependency. 101 Mobility's 194 territories and low SBA default rate (4.0%) suggest the model works. Median revenue is ~$490K; top quartile exceeds $1.47M. The franchise fee is $74K, total investment $182K to $362K. The question is whether the royalty (7%) and marketing fees buy enough lead flow to justify going franchise vs independent.
action: Request 101 Mobility FDD, call 3 franchisees (1 top-quartile, 1 median, 1 bottom-quartile), and compare independent startup economics.
why_works: Structural demographic tailwind (aging), needs-based not discretionary, insurance/Medicare reimbursement available, referral networks compound, low SBA default rate validates proven model.
why_fails: Referral dependency means slow ramp (12-18 months to build OT/PT/CM relationships), territory conflicts if 194 locations saturate markets, product margin compression from online DME retailers, and heavy inventory carrying costs for custom equipment.
validate_first: Call 3 franchisees across different quartiles and ask: how long did it take to break even? Would you do it again? What does the franchise provide that you could not do independently?
category_def: Medical equipment and mobility retail encompasses stair lifts, wheelchair ramps (modular and permanent), patient lifts, vehicle lifts, grab bars, and accessibility modifications. It sits at the intersection of home health care, DME (durable medical equipment) supply, and aging-in-place remodeling. Revenue comes from equipment sales (50-60% of revenue), installation labor (20-25%), and service/maintenance contracts (15-25%). The business is referral-driven: occupational therapists, case managers, hospital discharge planners, and VA coordinators are the gatekeepers. Medicare Part B may cover some equipment (patient lifts, certain ramps) if prescribed; stair lifts are typically private pay ($3,000 to $7,000 installed). The category includes both residential and light commercial (ADA compliance) work.
demand_drivers: Primary demand driver is demographics: 10,000 Americans turn 65 every day through 2029, and 73% want to age in place (AARP). Secondary drivers include hospital discharge planning (patients need home mods before release), VA benefits for veterans, and increasing ADA compliance requirements for commercial properties. The business is largely recession-resistant because it is needs-based — a post-surgery patient cannot defer a ramp installation. Recurrence comes from maintenance contracts on installed equipment and escalating needs (stair lift today, patient lift next year, bathroom modification the year after).
geo_fit: Best markets have high senior density (Florida, Northeast corridor, Upper Midwest). Miami/SFL is excellent due to retiree concentration. Boston/NE works for aging-in-place demand and hospital density. NY/CT/NJ is strong but high lease costs. Territory quality varies enormously — validate senior population density and hospital count within any proposed territory before committing.
core_buyer: Adult children (45-65) buying for aging parents; discharge planners arranging home modifications post-hospitalization; VA coordinators for veterans; OTs prescribing specific equipment after assessment
revenue_engine: Equipment sale + install leads to annual maintenance contract which feeds referral flywheel from OT/PT/CM network which generates repeat/expand revenue as patient needs escalate over time
hard_part: Building referral relationships takes 12-18 months. OTs and case managers are loyal to existing suppliers. You earn trust one successful install at a time. There is no shortcut — it is relationship farming, not relationship hunting.
why_path: Franchise is the default here because the product knowledge (stair lift brands, Medicare billing codes, ADA specs) and referral network take years to build independently. The franchise provides training, supplier relationships, and a recognized brand that OTs trust. However, an experienced DME dealer could go independent and save the 9% royalty/marketing drag. The comparison is close enough that both paths deserve modeling.
economics_intro: 101 Mobility's 2024 FDD Item 19 shows average per-territory sales from $238,918 (Q4) to $1,475,182 (Q1), with median around $490K. Franchise investment runs $182K to $362K including $74K franchise fee. At median revenue, operator EBITDA margins run 18-25% after royalty and marketing fees. The equipment carry cost and warehouse/showroom lease are the major fixed expenses. Independent operators save the 9% royalty/marketing but lose group purchasing power and brand recognition.
econ_insight: The most important number is the referral-to-close conversion rate. A franchisee with 15 active referral sources closing at 30% generates far more revenue than one with 50 sources closing at 5%. Track source attribution religiously.
econ_rows: Equipment vs install vs service split by territory
More than 70% equipment means margin risk from supply chain
Number of active referral sources and referral-to-close rate
Fewer than 5 referral sources means dangerous concentration
Percentage of installed base on annual contracts plus renewal rate
Less than 40% contract attach means leaving money on table
Turns below 4x means carrying too much stock
Below $2K/day/installer means routing or pricing problem
unit_econ_intro: Three-case unit economics model for a 101 Mobility franchise territory. Downside assumes 15% lower revenue, slower referral ramp, and one fewer installer. Base case reflects median franchisee performance. Upside assumes top-quartile territory with strong referral network and high maintenance contract attach rate.
buybox: Territory must have 100K+ seniors (65+) within 30-mile radius
At least 3 hospitals with active discharge planning departments
Existing DME competition is fragmented (no dominant player with more than 15% share)
VA medical center within territory for VA contract eligibility
Owner can commit to 12-month referral-building ramp before meaningful revenue
Franchisee satisfaction: confirm with at least 5 current franchisees before signing
disqualifiers: Territory with fewer than 50K seniors, only 1 hospital, and no VA center. Existing franchisee with pending litigation against franchisor. Territory where 2+ mobility dealers already have strong OT relationships. Medicare billing compliance issues.
competitive_intro: The mobility equipment market is fragmented between national DME chains (Numotion, NSM), local independent dealers, Amazon/DTC online sellers, and franchise networks (101 Mobility, Amramp). The competitive advantage is installation quality and referral relationships, not price. Customers are making a safety-critical purchase for a loved one; they trust the referral source, not the cheapest bid.
comp_whitespace_summary: Whitespace exists in mid-size metros (200-500K population) with no dedicated mobility dealer, underserved VA channels, after-hours emergency service (nobody offers it), and bundled smart-home aging-in-place packages. Most competitors are order-takers, not consultants.
comp_rows: Insurance contracts, broad product line, scale purchasing
Poor service quality, slow install times, no local relationship, high employee turnover
Deep referral networks, community trust, pricing flexibility
Limited product range, no brand recognition, succession risk, aging owners ready to retire
Price advantage, convenience for commodity items (grab bars, small ramps)
No installation service, no referral network, no Medicare billing capability, wrong product risk
Brand recognition, training, marketing system, referral support, group purchasing
Royalty drag (7-9%), territory restrictions, franchise compliance burden, cookie-cutter approach
valuation: Franchise resale: 2.5-4x SDE. Independent: 2-3x SDE. Top-quartile 101 Mobility territory ($1.47M revenue, ~$300K SDE) could sell for $750K-$1.2M. Median territory ($490K revenue, ~$100K SDE) sells for $250K-$400K. The referral network is the real asset — verify it transfers with the sale.
financing: SBA 7(a) loans are common (low 4% default rate signals lender comfort). 101 Mobility is on the SBA franchise registry. ROBS (401k rollover) also used for franchise fee. Equipment financing available for stair lift and ramp inventory. Seller financing possible on territory resales.
capital_rules: Never pay more than 3x verified SDE. Never assume you can improve margins by more than 5 percentage points. Budget for a 12-month transition where the seller is still involved. Model worst case at 80% of current revenue. The referral network is the asset — if it does not transfer, the price drops by 50%.
cust_diligence: Call 5 OTs/discharge planners: who do they refer mobility equipment to and why?
Ask referral sources: what would make you switch suppliers?
Check Medicare/Medicaid billing compliance history for any red flags
Verify insurance referral pipeline volume (monthly referral count by source)
Interview adult children buyers: what was their decision process?
oper_diligence: Installer training: how long, what certifications are needed?
Warehouse and inventory management system quality
Emergency service capability (ramp or lift breakdown means patient is stuck)
Staffing model: how many installers per $500K revenue?
Seasonality patterns (spike after hospital discharge seasons, flu season)
fin_diligence: Request 3 years of tax returns from any resale territory
Verify maintenance contract renewal rate (above 70% is good)
Check inventory aging report (older than 90 days is a problem)
Separate owner salary and perks from true SDE
Confirm all referral sources transfer with sale (get it in writing)
Review accounts receivable aging — Medicare payments can lag 60-90 days
growth_diligence: Adjacent product lines: bathroom modifications, vehicle lifts, ceiling lifts, vertical platform lifts
Commercial revenue opportunity: ADA compliance work for small businesses
VA contract expansion potential within territory
Partnership with home health agencies for bundled service offering
Smart-home aging-in-place technology integration (fall detection, medication reminders)
ai_intro: AI adds value in three places: lead capture (never miss a referral call), routing optimization (installer scheduling across territory), and CRM-driven reactivation (maintenance contract renewals). The referral-driven model means every missed call is a lost $3K-$7K job. An AI receptionist that captures after-hours referral calls could pay for itself in the first week.
ai_advantage: Lead capture and missed-call recovery is table stakes. Real advantage comes from predictive scheduling (estimating install time based on equipment type and home configuration) and automated reactivation campaigns triggered by warranty expiration dates. Most mobility dealers have zero CRM infrastructure — any system is a competitive advantage.
ai_rows: AI receptionist with call handling and text-back
Revenue captured per month vs prior period
AI scheduling optimization with dynamic dispatch
Jobs completed per installer per day increase
Automated CRM: warranty expiration alert then booking then confirm
CRM with source attribution and follow-up workflows
Photo-to-estimate AI for stair lift and ramp quotes
Demand forecasting based on referral pipeline
Inventory turns improvement, carrying cost reduction
risks: Referral dependency: 12-18 month ramp with minimal revenue while building OT/CM relationships — this is the number one risk
Insurance and Medicare billing complexity: claim denials, changing reimbursement codes, compliance audits
Product liability: improper stair lift or ramp installation can cause serious injury or death
Inventory carrying cost: stair lifts ($3-7K each) and modular ramps require warehouse space and working capital
Territory saturation: 194 locations means some markets have multiple franchisees competing
Succession risk: if franchisee exits, referral relationships may not transfer to buyer
Technology disruption: smart home and aging-in-place tech (stair-assist chairs, home elevators) could reduce traditional equipment demand
Franchisor risk: royalty increases, territory disputes, or franchisor financial problems
kill_criteria: Unable to confirm at least 3 franchisees who would do it again. Revenue declining year over year. Medicare compliance issues that cannot be resolved. Territory with fewer than 50K seniors. No viable path to positive cash flow within 18 months in the base case model.
market_stats: U.S. home accessibility and mobility market
U.S. stair lift and ramp market segment
Seniors who prefer to age in place
market_text: The home accessibility and mobility market is structurally driven by demographics: the 65+ population will grow from 56M to 73M by 2030. The age-in-place preference (73% of seniors per AARP) means home modifications are the default, not assisted living. Medicare spending on DME exceeds $10B annually. The market is fragmented with no single player having more than 5% national share. 101 Mobility and Amramp are the only national franchise brands in the mobility-specific space. Growth is steady at 6-8% annually, driven by aging demographics and increasing insurance coverage for home modifications. The COVID acceleration of home-based care created a permanent shift in discharge planning practices.
market_trend: Positive and structural. The aging demographic wave is the most predictable macro trend in the U.S. economy. Medicare Advantage plans are increasingly covering home modifications, expanding the addressable market. Technology is additive (smart-home integration) rather than disruptive to the core equipment/install model.
op_wins: Building deep OT/CM relationships creates a referral moat that is extremely hard to displace
Maintenance contracts create predictable recurring revenue on the installed base (target 60%+ attach rate)
VA contracts provide reliable government revenue stream with predictable payment cycles
Emergency and urgent installs (post-surgery, post-discharge) command premium pricing and build loyalty
Cross-selling naturally: stair lift customer becomes ramp customer becomes grab bar customer becomes bathroom mod customer
Referral sources compound over time: one happy OT refers to their entire department and to other departments
Low overhead model: warehouse + small showroom + 1-2 installers + owner-operator = lean cost structure
op_losses: Underestimating the 12-18 month referral ramp and burning through cash reserves while building relationships
Carrying too much inventory in slow-moving stair lift SKUs while running out of fast-moving ramp components
Hiring installers who lack patience and precision for elderly customer interactions — one bad experience kills the referral source
Ignoring maintenance contracts and leaving $50-100K/year of recurring revenue on the table
Competing on price against Amazon for commodity items instead of selling installation quality and trust
Not tracking referral source attribution and being unable to double down on what works
Taking on too much territory too fast before the referral network is solid in the core area
op_quote: The number one thing I hear from mobility equipment dealers: 'I wish I had started treating this like a real business from day one instead of winging it for the first two years.' The ones who invest in CRM, inventory management, and referral tracking from the start consistently outperform those who try to keep it all in their head.
comp_whitespace: Geographic whitespace: many mid-size metros (200-500K population) have no dedicated mobility equipment dealer — just general DME suppliers
Service gap: most DME companies are order-takers, not consultants. There is room for a concierge mobility advisor model that does home assessments and recommends holistic solutions
Commercial whitespace: ADA compliance work for small businesses (restaurants, retail, medical offices) is underserved by mobility-focused dealers
Bundling whitespace: combining mobility equipment with smart-home aging-in-place tech (fall detection sensors, medication reminders, voice-activated home controls)
VA whitespace: many territories under-serve the VA channel, which is a reliable payer with predictable volume
After-hours and weekend emergency service is almost never offered and could be a meaningful differentiator
Multi-family and senior living community bulk installations are an untapped channel for most dealers
score_yes: Structural demographic tailwind is real and accelerating — 10K/day turning 65
Needs-based, not discretionary demand is genuinely recession-resistant
Low SBA default rate (4.0%) validates the business model from a lender perspective
Recurring maintenance revenue provides stability and predictability
Franchise system is mature with 194 territories and years of operating history
score_caveats: 12-18 month referral ramp is a real cash burn risk that the score does not fully weight
Territory quality varies enormously — $239K to $1.47M revenue range is massive dispersion
Medicare/Medicaid billing adds regulatory complexity and compliance overhead
Inventory carry cost and warehouse lease are real fixed expenses that compress margins
The score does not capture the relationship-building patience and soft skills required
Franchise royalty of 9% total is a meaningful drag on smaller territories
experts: Revenue ramp timeline, actual referral conversion rates, royalty value perception, unexpected costs, support quality
What makes them refer (or not refer) a mobility supplier, switching triggers, common complaints about suppliers
Why they stayed independent, economics comparison with franchise, biggest competitive threat, margin reality
expert_calls: FDD contact list or franchisee referral
How long to reach $1M+ revenue? What drove your growth? Biggest mistake? Would you do it again? Support quality?
Is median revenue livable? How is the support? Territory adequacy? Biggest surprise?
Industry directories or Google Maps search
Why stay independent? Economics vs franchise? Biggest competitive threat? Margin reality?
Who do you refer and why? What would make you switch? Common complaints about suppliers? Volume per month?
roadmap30: Request 101 Mobility FDD and review Item 19 (financials), Item 6 (fees), Item 12 (territory) in detail
Call 5 franchisees (mix of quartiles) focusing on ramp timeline, support quality, and economics reality
Research territory availability in target markets (Boston/NE, NY/CT/NJ, Miami/SFL)
Talk to SBA lender about pre-qualification for franchise loan and understand terms
Visit a local 101 Mobility or Amramp operation to see the warehouse setup and meet the team
Start relationship with 2-3 OTs to understand referral dynamics from their perspective
kill30: If franchisees universally report dissatisfaction, or if no attractive territories are available in target markets, pause and reassess.
roadmap60: Build independent vs franchise side-by-side financial model with three-case scenarios
Map referral sources in target territory: hospitals, VA centers, rehab centers, home health agencies
Evaluate territory demographics: senior population density, competitor landscape, median income, hospital count
Negotiate territory if proceeding with franchise; confirm boundaries, exclusivity, and protection radius
If considering independent path: source stair lift and ramp suppliers, compare wholesale pricing to franchise GPO rates
Secure SBA pre-approval and identify warehouse/showroom lease options in target territory
kill60: If financial model shows negative cash flow in base case for more than 18 months, or independent path is clearly superior to franchise, pivot accordingly.
roadmap90: Make franchise vs independent decision with full financial conviction — no remaining loose ends
If franchise: sign agreement, begin training program, secure territory and start OT outreach
If independent: file business formation, secure supplier agreements, begin building OT referral relationships
Hire first installer (experienced, patient, excellent elderly-customer-service skills, clean background)
Set up CRM with referral source tracking, call handling, and maintenance contract management from day one
Launch referral outreach program targeting OTs, discharge planners, VA coordinators, and home health agencies
kill90: If unable to identify a concrete path to positive cash flow within 18 months, shelve the category and move to the next dashboard item.
final_rec: 101 Mobility is one of the stronger franchise plays in the dashboard. The demographic tailwind is undeniable, the SBA default rate is low, and the referral-driven moat is real once established. The key risk is the 12-18 month referral ramp — Dan needs to be comfortable funding operations during that period. If he can secure a territory with high senior density, multiple hospitals, and a VA center, this is a Pursue. Compare carefully with the independent path — the 9% royalty needs to buy real lead flow and purchasing power that justifies the drag. This is a long-term compounding play, not a quick flip.
next_actions: Request 101 Mobility FDD and do a deep Item 19 review
Call 5 franchisees across different revenue quartiles
Map target territory demographics (senior density, hospitals, VA centers)
Build franchise vs independent financial model
Make pursue/watch/pass decision with 90-day diligence budget
dan_decision: Is the mobility equipment franchise path worth 90 days of focused diligence right now, or should it go on the watchlist while pursuing the acquisition-path compliance categories (kitchen hood, radon, grease trap) that have faster time-to-revenue?
